quizzes.title: 2.1 Physical Quantities, Units and Prefixes

questions.prompt: Which of these is an SI base unit?
The unit of force, the newton, expressed in SI base units is:
The kelvin is an SI base unit.
Express 5.2 megaohms in ohms, using standard form.
State the SI base unit of electric current.
An equation states that pressure = force ÷ area. Using SI base units, the units of pressure are:

quizzes.title: 2.2 Measurements, Uncertainty and Error Analysis

questions.prompt: A systematic error causes readings to:
options: Be randomly scattered around the true value
Vary due to human reaction time
Be consistently shifted from the true value in one direction

questions.prompt: A length is measured as 24.0 cm using a ruler with a smallest division of 1 mm. The absolute uncertainty is best given as:
Random errors can be reduced by taking repeat readings and calculating a mean.
A current of (2.0 ± 0.1) A flows through a resistor. Calculate the percentage uncertainty in the current.
A resistor has resistance (10.0 ± 0.5) Ω and carries a current of (2.0 ± 0.1) A, each with 5% uncertainty. Estimate the percentage uncertainty in the power dissipated, P = I²R.
Which combination rule is correct when multiplying two quantities together to calculate their combined percentage uncertainty?

quizzes.title: 2.3 Scalars and Vectors

questions.prompt: Which of these is a vector quantity?
A force of 10 N acts at 30° above the horizontal. Calculate the horizontal component (2 s.f.).
The resultant of two vectors can be found by placing them head-to-tail and drawing the vector from the start of the first to the end of the second.
A force of 10 N acts at 30° above the horizontal. Calculate the vertical component (2 s.f.).
What is meant by a scalar quantity?
acceptedAnswers: a quantity with magnitude only, no direction

questions.prompt: Two forces, 3 N and 4 N, act at right angles to each other. The magnitude of their resultant is:

quizzes.title: 3.1 Motion: Displacement, Velocity, Acceleration and SUVAT

questions.prompt: Velocity is defined as:
options: Distance per unit time in a straight line

questions.prompt: A car accelerates uniformly from rest to 20 m/s in 8.0 s. Calculate its acceleration.
Displacement is a scalar quantity.
A ball is thrown vertically upward with initial speed 15 m/s. Using g = 9.81 m/s², calculate the time taken to reach maximum height (2 s.f.).
Using s = ut + ½at², calculate the distance fallen by an object released from rest after 3.0 s (use g = 9.81 m/s², 2 s.f.).
Which suvat equation should be used to find final velocity when displacement, initial velocity and acceleration are known, but time is not?

quizzes.title: 3.2 Motion Graphs and Projectile Motion

questions.prompt: On a velocity–time graph, the gradient represents:
On a displacement–time graph, the area under the graph represents:
In projectile motion (ignoring air resistance), the horizontal and vertical components of motion are independent of each other.
A ball is projected horizontally from a height of 5.0 m. Using g = 9.81 m/s², calculate the time taken to reach the ground (2 s.f.).
A ball is projected horizontally at 12 m/s and falls for 1.0 s before landing. Calculate the horizontal distance travelled.
For a projectile launched at an angle above the horizontal (ignoring air resistance), the vertical component of velocity at the highest point of the trajectory is:

quizzes.title: 3.3 Forces in Action: Newton's Laws and Free-Body Diagrams

questions.prompt: Newton's first law states that an object will remain at rest or move with constant velocity unless:
options: It experiences any force at all

questions.prompt: A resultant force of 15 N acts on a mass of 3.0 kg. Calculate the acceleration produced.
A free-body diagram shows every force acting on an object, but not the forces the object exerts on other objects.
A resultant force of 24 N gives a trolley an acceleration of 3.0 m/s². Calculate the mass of the trolley.
State Newton's second law of motion in terms of resultant force, mass and acceleration.
acceptedAnswers: resultant force equals mass times acceleration (F = ma)

questions.prompt: A book rests on a table. Which pair of forces is an example of a Newton's third law pair?
options: The weight of the book and the normal contact force from the table
The force of the book on the table and the force of the table on the book
The weight of the book and the weight of the table
Friction and the normal force on the book

quizzes.title: 3.4 Moments, Equilibrium and Density/Pressure

questions.prompt: The moment of a force about a point is defined as:
options: Force × perpendicular distance from the point to the line of action of the force

questions.prompt: A force of 20 N acts perpendicular to a spanner at a distance of 0.15 m from a nut. Calculate the moment.
For a body in equilibrium, the sum of clockwise moments about any point equals the sum of anticlockwise moments about that same point.
Calculate the density of an object with mass 540 g and volume 200 cm³, in kg/m³.
Calculate the pressure exerted by a force of 450 N acting over an area of 0.030 m².
The pressure at depth h in a fluid of density ρ is given by P = hρg. If both depth and density are doubled, the pressure:

quizzes.title: 3.5 Work, Energy and Power

questions.prompt: Work done by a force is defined as:
options: Force × distance moved in the direction of the force

questions.prompt: Calculate the work done when a force of 25 N moves an object 4.0 m in the direction of the force.
Power is defined as the rate of doing work, measured in watts.
Calculate the power required to do 600 J of work in 12 s.
Calculate the kinetic energy of a 2.0 kg object moving at 6.0 m/s.
A car's engine provides a constant driving force while the car travels at constant velocity against resistive forces. Which statement is correct?
options: The driving force is greater than the resistive force
The driving force equals the resistive force

quizzes.title: 3.6 Conservation of Energy and Efficiency

questions.prompt: The principle of conservation of energy states that:
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred or transformed from one form to another
Energy is always converted to heat
Energy can be created but not destroyed

questions.prompt: A ball of mass 0.50 kg is dropped from a height of 2.0 m. Calculate its speed just before hitting the ground (ignore air resistance, g = 9.81 m/s², 2 s.f.).
Efficiency can be greater than 100% for a real machine.
A motor does 800 J of useful work while consuming 1000 J of energy. Calculate its efficiency as a percentage.
Calculate the gravitational potential energy gained by a 5.0 kg mass raised 3.0 m (g = 9.81 m/s²).
In a system where energy is transferred from gravitational potential energy to kinetic energy with no other losses, which statement is true at any point during the fall?

quizzes.title: 3.7 Materials: Hooke's Law, Stress, Strain and the Young Modulus

questions.prompt: Hooke's law states that:
options: Extension is proportional to the square of the force
Stress is inversely proportional to strain
Extension is proportional to applied force, provided the elastic limit is not exceeded

questions.prompt: A spring has a spring constant of 40 N/m. Calculate the force needed to extend it by 0.05 m.
The Young modulus is defined as stress divided by strain, and applies within the region where a material obeys Hooke's law.
A wire of cross-sectional area 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m² is under a tension of 40 N. Calculate the stress.
A wire of original length 2.0 m extends by 1.0 mm. Calculate the strain (no unit).
A wire has stress 4.0 × 10⁷ Pa and strain 5.0 × 10⁻⁴. Calculate the Young modulus of the wire.

quizzes.title: 3.8 Newton's Third Law, Momentum and Collisions

questions.prompt: Linear momentum is defined as:
Calculate the momentum of a 1200 kg car travelling at 15 m/s.
In any collision, provided no external forces act, total momentum is conserved even if kinetic energy is not.
A trolley of mass 2.0 kg moving at 3.0 m/s collides and sticks to a stationary trolley of mass 1.0 kg. Calculate the common velocity after the collision.
State the principle of conservation of momentum.
acceptedAnswers: the total momentum of a system is conserved provided no external force acts
total momentum before equals total momentum after, if no external force acts

questions.prompt: In a perfectly elastic collision between two objects:
options: Both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved

quizzes.title: 4.1 Charge, Current and Potential Difference

questions.prompt: Electric current is defined as:
Calculate the charge that flows when a current of 2.5 A flows for 40 s.
Potential difference is defined as energy transferred per unit charge.
Calculate the current if 300 C of charge flows in 60 s.
Calculate the energy transferred when a charge of 15 C flows through a potential difference of 12 V.
In a metallic conductor, conventional current flows:
options: In the same direction as electron flow
In the direction opposite to electron flow

quizzes.title: 4.2 Resistance, Resistivity and I–V Characteristics

questions.prompt: Resistance is defined by the equation:
Calculate the resistance of a component with a p.d. of 12 V and current of 0.5 A.
The resistance of a filament lamp increases as its temperature increases.
A wire has resistivity 1.7 × 10⁻⁸ Ω m, length 2.0 m, and cross-sectional area 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m². Calculate its resistance.
State the equation defining resistivity ρ in terms of resistance R, length L and cross-sectional area A.
Which I–V graph feature indicates an ohmic conductor at constant temperature?
options: A curve through the origin with increasing gradient
A curve that flattens as V increases
A straight line through the origin

quizzes.title: 4.3 Circuits: EMF, Internal Resistance and Kirchhoff's Laws

questions.prompt: The electromotive force (EMF) of a source is defined as:
options: The current supplied by the source
The potential difference across the terminals when current flows
The resistance of the internal components
The energy transferred per unit charge by the source, including internal losses

questions.prompt: A cell of EMF 6.0 V and internal resistance 0.50 Ω drives a current of 2.0 A through an external resistor. Calculate the terminal p.d.
Kirchhoff's second law is a statement of conservation of energy around a closed loop.
A cell of internal resistance 0.50 Ω delivers a current of 2.0 A. Calculate the power dissipated inside the cell.
State Kirchhoff's first law.
acceptedAnswers: the sum of currents entering a junction equals the sum of currents leaving it
total current into a junction equals total current out

questions.prompt: Two resistors of 4 Ω and 12 Ω are connected in parallel. Calculate the combined resistance.

quizzes.title: 4.4 The Potential Divider

questions.prompt: A potential divider is used to:
options: Increase the total current in a circuit
Produce a variable or fixed fraction of a source's voltage
Produce a constant current regardless of load

questions.prompt: Two resistors, 20 Ω and 60 Ω, are connected in series across a 12 V supply as a potential divider. Calculate the voltage across the 60 Ω resistor.
A light-dependent resistor (LDR) has a resistance that decreases as light intensity increases.
A potential divider has resistors of 10 Ω and 30 Ω in series across a 20 V supply. Calculate the voltage across the 10 Ω resistor.
State how the resistance of a standard (NTC) thermistor changes as its temperature increases.

In a potential divider circuit using an LDR and a fixed resistor, as light intensity increases the LDR's resistance decreases. If the output voltage is taken across the fixed resistor, the output voltage will:

quizzes.title: 4.5 Progressive Waves and Wave Properties

questions.prompt: The wave equation relates wave speed, frequency and wavelength as:
A wave has frequency 50 Hz and wavelength 4.0 m. Calculate its speed.
The frequency of a wave is defined as the number of complete oscillations per unit time.
A wave travels at 340 m/s with a frequency of 680 Hz. Calculate its wavelength.
Define the amplitude of a wave.
acceptedAnswers: the maximum displacement from the equilibrium (rest) position

questions.prompt: Two points on a wave are said to be in phase when:
options: They move in opposite directions at all times
They are exactly half a wavelength apart
One leads the other by a quarter wavelength
They have the same displacement and velocity at all times

quizzes.title: 4.6 Longitudinal and Transverse Waves, Polarisation

questions.prompt: In a transverse wave, the oscillations of particles are:
options: Perpendicular to the direction of energy transfer
Parallel to the direction of energy transfer
At 45° to the direction of energy transfer

questions.prompt: Which of these is an example of a longitudinal wave?
Only transverse waves can be polarised.
State what is meant by polarisation of a wave.
acceptedAnswers: restricting the oscillations of a transverse wave to a single plane
oscillations confined to one plane, perpendicular to the direction of travel

questions.prompt: Name one everyday application that relies on the polarisation of light.
A wave passes through a polarising filter and then a second filter at 90° to the first. What is observed?

quizzes.title: 4.7 Superposition, Standing Waves and Two-Source Interference

questions.prompt: The principle of superposition states that:
options: Waves always cancel when they meet
When two waves meet, the resultant displacement equals the vector sum of the individual displacements

questions.prompt: A stationary (standing) wave is formed when:
options: Two waves of the same frequency and amplitude travelling in opposite directions superpose
A single wave reflects off a fixed boundary only
Two waves of different frequencies meet
Energy is transferred continuously along the wave

questions.prompt: At a node in a standing wave, the amplitude of oscillation is zero.
State what is meant by an antinode in a standing wave pattern.
acceptedAnswers: a point of maximum amplitude of oscillation
point where the wave oscillates with maximum amplitude

questions.prompt: A standing wave has adjacent nodes 0.60 m apart, vibrating at 50 Hz. Calculate the wave speed.
For two coherent sources, constructive interference occurs at points where the path difference is:
options: A half-integer number of wavelengths ((n + ½)λ)

quizzes.title: 4.8 Young's Double-Slit Experiment and Diffraction Gratings

questions.prompt: In Young's double-slit experiment, fringe spacing w is related to wavelength λ, slit separation s, and distance to screen D by:
Light of wavelength 600 nm passes through slits 0.50 mm apart, forming fringes on a screen 2.0 m away. Calculate the fringe spacing.
A diffraction grating with more lines per millimetre produces a wider angular separation between diffraction orders, for a given wavelength.
A diffraction grating has 300 lines per mm. Calculate the grating spacing, d.
A diffraction grating has spacing 3.3 × 10⁻⁶ m. Using dsinθ = nλ, calculate the angle of the first-order maximum for light of wavelength 500 nm (2 s.f.).
Compared to a double slit, a diffraction grating with many slits produces:

quizzes.title: 4.9 The Photoelectric Effect and Photon Energy

questions.prompt: The photoelectric effect provides evidence that light:
options: Behaves as discrete packets of energy called photons
Travels slower in a vacuum than in glass

questions.prompt: Calculate the energy of a photon of frequency 5.0 × 10¹⁴ Hz (h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s).
The photoelectric effect shows that below the threshold frequency, no photoelectrons are emitted no matter how intense the light.
Calculate the energy, in electron-volts, of a photon with energy 4.8 × 10⁻¹⁹ J (1 eV = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ J).
Define the work function of a metal.
acceptedAnswers: the minimum energy needed to remove an electron from the surface of a metal
minimum energy required to release an electron from a metal's surface

questions.prompt: In the photoelectric equation hf = Φ + KEmax, increasing the frequency of incident light (above the threshold) while keeping intensity constant causes:
options: The number of photoelectrons emitted per second to increase
The maximum kinetic energy of emitted electrons to increase

quizzes.title: 4.10 Wave-Particle Duality and Electron Diffraction

questions.prompt: The de Broglie wavelength of a particle is given by:

Electron diffraction is evidence that particles can show wave-like behaviour.
Calculate the momentum of a particle with de Broglie wavelength 2.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ m (h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s).
Name the phenomenon that provides evidence for the wave nature of particles such as electrons.

quizzes.title: 5.1 Thermal Physics: Temperature, Specific Heat Capacity and Latent Heat

questions.prompt: Specific heat capacity is defined as:
options: The total heat content of an object
The energy required to melt 1 kg of a substance
The energy required to raise the temperature of 1 g of a substance by 1°C
The energy required to raise the temperature of 1 kg of a substance by 1 K

questions.prompt: Calculate the energy needed to raise the temperature of 2.0 kg of water (c = 4200 J/kg/K) by 15 K.
Specific latent heat is the energy required to change the state of 1 kg of a substance without any change in temperature.
Calculate the energy required to melt 0.50 kg of ice (specific latent heat of fusion of ice = 3.34 × 10⁵ J/kg).
State the difference between specific latent heat of fusion and specific latent heat of vaporisation.
acceptedAnswers: fusion is for melting/freezing, vaporisation is for boiling/condensing
fusion refers to solid-liquid change, vaporisation refers to liquid-gas change

questions.prompt: During a change of state at constant temperature, the energy supplied to a substance:
options: Increases the kinetic energy of particles
Increases the potential energy between particles, not their kinetic energy
Increases both kinetic and potential energy equally

quizzes.title: 5.2 Ideal Gases and the Kinetic Theory of Gases

questions.prompt: The ideal gas equation is given by:
Calculate the pressure of 2.0 mol of an ideal gas in a volume of 0.010 m³ at a temperature of 300 K (R = 8.31 J/mol/K).
The kinetic theory model assumes that collisions between gas molecules are perfectly elastic.
Calculate the number of moles of gas in a container of volume 0.020 m³ at pressure 2.0 × 10⁵ Pa and temperature 350 K (R = 8.31 J/mol/K, 2 s.f.).
State one assumption of the kinetic theory of gases regarding the volume of gas molecules.
acceptedAnswers: the volume of the molecules is negligible compared to the volume of the container
molecules have negligible volume compared to the gas

questions.prompt: According to kinetic theory, the average kinetic energy of gas molecules is directly proportional to:

quizzes.title: 5.3 Circular Motion

questions.prompt: Angular speed ω is related to the period T by:
Calculate the angular speed of an object completing one revolution every 4.0 s (2 s.f.).
An object moving in a circle at constant speed has a constant velocity.
Calculate the centripetal acceleration of an object moving in a circle of radius 0.50 m at a speed of 4.0 m/s.
State the direction of the centripetal force acting on an object moving in a circular path.
acceptedAnswers: towards the centre of the circle

questions.prompt: A ball on a string moves in a horizontal circle of radius r at angular speed ω. If both r and ω are doubled, the centripetal acceleration (a = ω²r) becomes:

quizzes.title: 5.4 Simple Harmonic Motion: Definition and the SHM Equations

questions.prompt: Simple harmonic motion is defined as motion in which:
options: Acceleration is proportional to displacement from equilibrium and directed towards it

questions.prompt: A mass on a spring oscillates with SHM of amplitude 0.20 m and angular frequency 5.0 rad/s. Calculate the maximum speed.
For SHM, the time period is independent of amplitude.
Calculate the maximum acceleration of an oscillator with amplitude 0.20 m and angular frequency 5.0 rad/s.
State the equation for the period of a mass-spring system in terms of mass m and spring constant k.
acceptedAnswers: T equals 2 pi times the square root of m over k

questions.prompt: For an object undergoing SHM, the acceleration is maximum when the displacement is:

quizzes.title: 5.5 Energy in SHM, Damping and Resonance

questions.prompt: In SHM (ignoring damping), the total mechanical energy of the oscillator:
options: Is maximum at the equilibrium position only
Is zero at the equilibrium position

questions.prompt: A mass-spring oscillator has spring constant 50 N/m and amplitude 0.10 m. Calculate the maximum (total) energy of the oscillation.
Critical damping returns a system to equilibrium in the shortest possible time without oscillating.
State what is meant by resonance.
acceptedAnswers: when a system is driven at its natural frequency, causing the amplitude of oscillation to become maximum
large amplitude oscillation when driving frequency equals natural frequency

questions.prompt: Name one real-world example where damping is deliberately increased to reduce unwanted oscillations.
As damping in an oscillating system increases, the resonant peak on a graph of amplitude against driving frequency:
options: Shifts to a higher frequency only

quizzes.title: 5.6 Gravitational Fields and Newton's Law of Gravitation

questions.prompt: Newton's law of gravitation states that the force between two point masses is:
options: Inversely proportional to the product of their masses
Directly proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of their separation

questions.prompt: Calculate the gravitational force between two masses of 5.0 kg and 3.0 kg separated by 0.20 m (G = 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ N m² kg⁻², 2 s.f.).
Gravitational field strength is defined as the gravitational force per unit mass.
Calculate the gravitational field strength at the surface of a planet of mass 6.0 × 10²⁴ kg and radius 6.4 × 10⁶ m (G = 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹, 2 s.f.).
State the equation for gravitational field strength g at a distance r from a point mass M.
acceptedAnswers: g equals G M over r squared

questions.prompt: If the distance between two masses is tripled, the gravitational force between them becomes:

quizzes.title: 5.7 Gravitational Potential, Orbits and Kepler's Laws

questions.prompt: Gravitational potential at a point is defined as:
options: The force per unit mass at that point
The work done per unit mass to move a small test mass from infinity to that point
The total energy of an orbiting satellite
The escape velocity at that point

questions.prompt: A satellite orbits a planet of mass 6.0 × 10²⁴ kg at a radius of 7.0 × 10⁶ m. Using v = √(GM/r) (G = 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹), calculate its orbital speed (2 s.f.).
Kepler's third law states that the square of a planet's orbital period is proportional to the cube of its orbital radius (T² ∝ r³).
State what is meant by a geostationary orbit.
acceptedAnswers: an orbit above the equator with a period of 24 hours, so the satellite stays above the same point on Earth
24-hour equatorial orbit matching Earth's rotation, satellite appears stationary

questions.prompt: Planet A has orbital radius r and period T. Planet B orbits the same star with radius 4r. Using Kepler's third law (T² ∝ r³), find planet B's period in terms of T.
For a satellite in a stable circular orbit, the gravitational force provides:
options: A force that increases the satellite's speed continuously
A force that acts tangentially to the orbit
The centripetal force needed to maintain the circular orbit

quizzes.title: 5.8 Stars: Classification, Luminosity and the Hertzsprung-Russell Diagram

questions.prompt: The luminosity of a star is defined as:
options: The total energy emitted per unit time (power output)
The energy received per unit area at Earth
The surface temperature of the star
The star's apparent brightness as seen from Earth

questions.prompt: On a Hertzsprung-Russell (H-R) diagram, most stars (including the Sun) lie on a band called:
Stefan's law states that the luminosity of a star is proportional to the fourth power of its surface temperature, for a given radius.
Using Stefan's law L = 4πr²σT⁴ (σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W m⁻² K⁻⁴), calculate the luminosity of a star with radius 7.0 × 10⁸ m and surface temperature 5800 K (2 s.f.).
State what is meant by a black-body radiator.
acceptedAnswers: an idealised object that absorbs all incident radiation and emits a characteristic spectrum depending only on its temperature
a perfect absorber and emitter of radiation, with emission spectrum depending only on temperature

questions.prompt: Wien's law states that the wavelength at peak intensity λmax is inversely proportional to temperature. If a star's temperature doubles, λmax becomes:

quizzes.title: 5.9 Telescopes and the Electromagnetic Spectrum in Astronomy

questions.prompt: The angular resolution of a telescope is limited by:
options: The colour of the object being observed

questions.prompt: Using the Rayleigh criterion θ ≈ λ/D, calculate the angular resolution (in radians) of a telescope with aperture 2.0 m observing light of wavelength 550 nm.
A larger telescope aperture improves both the light-gathering power and the angular resolution.

acceptedAnswers: mirrors don't suffer from chromatic aberration, and large mirrors are easier to support than large lenses

questions.prompt: Name one region of the electromagnetic spectrum, other than visible light, used by astronomers to observe distant objects.
Observing in the radio part of the spectrum rather than visible light generally requires:
options: A much smaller aperture for the same resolution
A much larger dish/aperture to achieve comparable angular resolution
The same aperture as an optical telescope for equivalent resolution

quizzes.title: 5.10 Cosmology: Doppler Redshift, Hubble's Law and the Big Bang

questions.prompt: Cosmological redshift of light from distant galaxies provides evidence that:

State what is meant by the cosmic microwave background (CMB) radiation.
acceptedAnswers: the remnant thermal radiation left over from the early, hot universe, now redshifted into the microwave region
relic radiation from shortly after the Big Bang, now observed as microwave radiation filling the universe

questions.prompt: According to Hubble's law, a galaxy twice as far away as another (assuming the same expansion rate) will have a recession velocity that is:

questions.prompt: Capacitance is defined by the equation:

The energy stored in a charged capacitor is given by E = ½QV.

charge decays exponentially with time constant RC

quizzes.title: 6.2 Electric Fields and Coulomb's Law

acceptedAnswers: field strength equals voltage divided by plate separation

quizzes.title: 6.3 Magnetic Fields and the Motor Effect

questions.prompt: The force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is given by:

The direction of the force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field can be found using Fleming's left-hand rule.
Calculate the force on a proton (charge 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C) moving at 2.0 × 10⁶ m/s perpendicular to a magnetic field of 0.50 T.

acceptedAnswers: force equals magnetic flux density times charge times velocity

questions.prompt: A positive charge moves through a magnetic field with velocity parallel to the field lines. The magnetic force on the charge is:

options: Inversely proportional to the rate of change of flux
Proportional to the magnetic flux linkage itself
Proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux linkage
Always zero in a changing field

questions.prompt: A coil of 200 turns experiences a change in flux of 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ Wb in 0.20 s. Calculate the induced EMF.
Lenz's law states that the direction of an induced EMF is such that it opposes the change producing it.
A transformer has 100 turns on the primary coil and 500 turns on the secondary. If the primary voltage is 12 V, calculate the secondary voltage (assume an ideal transformer).

quizzes.title: 6.5 Alternating Current

questions.prompt: The root-mean-square (rms) value of an alternating current is defined as the value of direct current that:
options: Is equal to the peak current
Would produce the same average power (heating effect) in a resistor
Is always half the peak current

questions.prompt: Calculate the rms voltage of an AC supply with peak voltage 325 V (2 s.f.).
For a sinusoidal AC supply, Irms = Ipeak/√2.
Calculate the peak current for an AC supply with rms current of 3.0 A (2 s.f.).
State why the rms value, rather than the peak value, is used to describe the 'effective' value of the household AC mains supply.
acceptedAnswers: it gives the equivalent steady (DC) value that would deliver the same average power
because it represents the DC equivalent for power/heating effect

questions.prompt: The mains supply in the UK has an rms voltage of about 230 V. The peak voltage is therefore approximately:

quizzes.title: 6.6 Particle Physics: Classification of Particles, Quarks and Leptons

questions.prompt: Hadrons are particles that:
options: Are made of quarks and experience the strong nuclear force
Are fundamental particles with no substructure

questions.prompt: Which of these is a lepton?
Baryons (such as protons and neutrons) are made of three quarks.
State the quark composition of a proton, in terms of up (u) and down (d) quarks.
acceptedAnswers: two up quarks and one down quark

questions.prompt: State the quark composition of a neutron.
acceptedAnswers: one up quark and two down quarks

questions.prompt: Mesons are hadrons made up of:

quizzes.title: 6.7 Particle Interactions and Conservation Laws

questions.prompt: In particle interactions, which quantities must always be conserved?
options: Charge, baryon number and lepton number

questions.prompt: Which fundamental force is responsible for beta decay?
In beta-minus decay, a neutron changes into a proton, an electron and an electron antineutrino.
State the particle emitted alongside the electron in beta-minus decay, needed to conserve lepton number and energy.
State one quantity, other than energy and momentum, that must be conserved in all particle interactions.
When a particle and its antiparticle meet, they can undergo:
options: Fusion into a single stable particle
Annihilation, producing photons (or other particle-antiparticle pairs)

quizzes.title: 6.8 Radioactive Decay and Half-Life

questions.prompt: The half-life of a radioactive isotope is defined as:
options: The time taken for half the mass to disappear as energy
The time taken for all nuclei to decay
The time taken for the activity (or number of undecayed nuclei) to halve
A constant value that is the same for all isotopes

questions.prompt: A sample has an initial activity of 800 Bq and a half-life of 10 days. Calculate the activity after 30 days.
Radioactive decay is a random process, meaning it is impossible to predict exactly when a particular nucleus will decay.
The decay constant λ is related to half-life T½ by λ = ln(2)/T½. Calculate the decay constant for an isotope with half-life 5.0 days (per day, 2 s.f.).
State the equation relating activity A, decay constant λ, and the number of undecayed nuclei N.
acceptedAnswers: activity equals decay constant times number of undecayed nuclei

questions.prompt: After 2 half-lives, the fraction of the original undecayed nuclei remaining is:

quizzes.title: 6.9 Nuclear Radius, Binding Energy and Medical Imaging

questions.prompt: The radius of a nucleus is related to its nucleon number A by:
Using R₀ = 1.2 fm, calculate the radius of a nucleus with nucleon number 27 (A^(1/3) = 3).
Nuclear binding energy is the energy required to separate a nucleus into its individual, unbound protons and neutrons.
Using E = mc², calculate the energy equivalent of a mass defect of 3.0 × 10⁻²⁹ kg (c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m/s).
Name one medical imaging technique that uses non-ionising radiation (as opposed to X-rays, which are ionising).
Compared to X-ray imaging, ultrasound imaging is generally considered:
options: More dangerous, since it penetrates further
Identical in terms of radiation risk
Safer, since it uses non-ionising radiation
